Ema ranyatingstéSgmalleon 2. Using common beans (Phaseolus vulgaris L) as the focal crop, this study tested
Wess amd whether a tropical agroforestry system called Chagga home gardens supports
Swed international Development pollinator communities and improves polination and crop yield. We examined 16

operation Agony Sl Grant Award

acme agroforestry plots paired with 16 non-agroforestry plots located along a gradient

'of woody (trees and shrubs) cover within a 1-km radius to document the abun
dance, species richness and visitation rates of polinating insects on bean flowers
and how they contributed to bean yield

3, The beans planted inthe agroforestry plots had almost twice the abundance of
Insect pollinators, three times the richness of the species and almost twice the
visitation rates than those planted in non-agroforestry plots. We also found asig-
nificant positive effect of woody coverin the surrounding landscape on insect pol-
linator abundances, but not on species richness and visitation rates. Additionally,
the abundance and richness of insect pollinators increased significantly with
flower abundance, while the overall plant richness in a plot significantly increased
insect pollinator abundance and thei visitation rate. The difference in bean yield
between unbagged flowers (in which insect pollinators were allowed to access
bean flowers) and bagged flowers and the total yield were higher in agroforestry
than in non-agroforestry plots.

4. Synthesis and opplicatios. This study showed that. compared to monocul-
ture, agroforestry generally promotes pollination services in a tropical context.
Furthermore, we found that agroforestry is likely to be particularly helpful for

1 | INTRODUCTION

(One-third of human fod is obtained fem plant species that depend
onpolinators for production Garitald et l.2011: Kitt tal. 2013
'in eta, 2007). Of al plinators, sects represent the most
portant sroup and ae the main crop pollinators in most agroeco-
systems (Abou-Shanra, 2036; Paiclo-Raberta & Campos, 2014),
Unfortunately, a sgnifieant dedine in these cruel Insects has
been observed globally as a result of agricultural Intenstcation,
climate change and loss of habitat (Outhwaite et al, 2022; Potts
etal, 2010; Sdocher Bayo & Wyckhuys. 2019: Wagner et al. 2021)
Consequently, this has negatively affected global food production
ad security (Centeno-Alvarado etal 2023; Grab et al, 2019; Potts
etal, 2016) nrecene years, there has been an effort to reverse this
negative trend mainy by implementing eclogcatintervetions that
im at diverting agricultural landscapes (eg. agroforestry Hower
strips Dicks eta, 2016; IPRES, 2016; Pots et al, 2016; Vanbergen,
et a, 2020) they are assumed benefit polnating insect (bell,
tt a, 2017; Kovies Hostyinski eta, 2017) Studies have shown
that these ecological interventions usually but not always have
postive eects on insect poliators (bell et al. 2017; Scheper
etal, 2019,

Agroforestry, which involves the integration of woody plants
(trees and shrubs) with crops and/or livestock on the same piece
land, san eclogiea intervention that has the potenti to sup-
port potinators (Bentrup et at. 2019), Agroforestry Increases the
'overall plant diversity and structural complexity In the landscape
that provides adtional food and nesting resources to insect pol
lnators (ose, 2008). Woody plants In agrofeesty systems pro-
vide alternative forage for insect polinators when crops are notin
loom or attract pollinators towards unattractive crops (Kremen
fetal, 2007; Martins Salinas etal. 2022, Woody plants can also
provide shelter against environmental extremes and agricultural
lsturbances (Bentrup et a, 2019: Centeno-Alvarado etal, 2023)
Some studies have shown that agroforestry Increases the versity
and abundance of insect poinators, because it offers more food
and nesting resources for insect pollinators Hoch et al, 2010; Siva
[Neto et a. 2024; Varah at al, 2020). At the same time, athe stud
les found no effect of agroforestry on polinator diversity Knowlton
tal. 2019; Tarigan etal 2021) and occasionally evenalower polina-
tor abundance tha conventional agree (Galbraith et al, 2020}
Most studies predicting the impact of agroforestry on insect pol
Inators have been conducted in temperate Europe and America
(Centeno-Alvarado etal, 2028;Timberake & Morgan, 2018), which

'sunkely to be globally representative and feaves the impact of
_agrofrests in troplca East Ate arely unknown,

'There are several potential reasons why agroforestry does not
sways effectively support poliators. One is that tree diversity
{in many agroforestey systems i low or ayofotestry may be prac-
ticed on 2 too-small spatial scale, and therefore, the amoust of
floral resources or nest sites may not increase much compared to
'non-agrotorestry farms (Knowiton etal. 2019: Tagan et al, 202%),
Another reason may be the composition of surrounding landscapes

whereby landscape homogenization, s well as fragmentation and
lose of natural habitat. has been shown to affect the abundance
and versity of insect polinstors: ait reduces thele food and
nest resources availabilty (Dainese et al, 2019; Klein etal. 2012
'Therefore, the lndscape context san important factor to consider
when evaluating the effect of ecological interventions such 35a
{oestry on insect pollinators

shade and fodder provision Hemp, 2005: Mbeyale& Mehar, 2022),

4 | DISCUSSION

'As expected, we found that the abundance, species richness and vis
Itation rate of insect polinators visting bean flowers were higher in
agroforestry plots than in non-aroforestry plots and this esuited
In an increased number of seeds per pod when pollinators had ac-
'cess to bean lowers. Tiss compeling evidence that agroforestry
'an support a higher number of insect polinators and thus improve
polination services compare to non- agroforestry farming systems,
Previous work has shown variable results on the efect of agrofor-
'|stry on insect polinators and polination services. Several studies
have fund that agroforestry increases the diversity and abundance
of insect polinators. and thus pollination services. (Bentrup
etal, 2019; Centeno-AWvarado eta, 2023: Varah et a, 2020) At
'the same time, other studies found tht agroforestry id not affect
polinator diversity (Krowiton et al, 2039; Tarigan et al, 2021) and
'occasionally had lower polinato abundance than conventions
'culture (Galbraith eta, 2020), Our resus support the argument

that agroforestry benefits insect polnatrs and thus imorovespol-
tinton services.

'When analysing the potential mechanisms underlying. the
positive effects of agroforestry, we found that flower abun>
ance which was signfieantly higher In agroforestry than non
agroforestry plots (Table $2) had a positive effect on insect
polinator abundance and species richness (Figures 2C and 2). The
flower abundance inthe apricuituraliandscape i often positively
correlated with the abundance and richness of insect pollinators.
For example this pattern was observed when plots with enhanced
Wwildlower platings were compared with control plots (non-
enhanced) in agricultural areas (Blaauw 6 Isan's, 2016; Campbell
cal, 2029: Isbell tal, 2017 Suter etal. 2017). Likewise. sev:
'era studies have reported strong local scale response of insect
polinator abundance to flower abundance mediated by greater

plant richness (Hoptenmiller et al, 2014: Lichtenbere et a, 2037:
Scheper et al, 2015). We aso found thatthe overall plant rch:
ness significantly increased insect pollinator richness and visit
tion rate, This suggests that agroforestry plats donot only support
polinators through increased svallbity of floral resources, but
also by providing nest sites and shelter (Delaney et a. 2020:
Schuldt et al, 2019). More specially, lant speces richness may
support polinators (1) by providing a variety of nesting resources.

(2) by providing floral resources over an extended period due to
'non-synchronous flowering, (3) by providing a wide range of ml
'rocimatic conditions that butfers extreme temperatures and of-
fers shelter against rain and (8) by increasing the likelihood that,
plant species are present that sre prefered by different poli
tors (Schult eta, 2019: Vanbergen eta, 2020). This argument
\s supported by several previous studies showing that trees and
shrubs in agroforestry plots provide addtional floral resources,

and nesting resources that support the growth and persistence
of insect pollinators (Bentrup et al. 2019; CentenoAlvarado
et a, 2025; Kay etal, 2020).

We found that polinator abundance significantly increased
With the covet of trees and shrubs in the surrounding landscape
The significant postive effect of woody covers hely de to its
contribution to insect plinators' forage and/or nesting resources
(Watson etal, 2013; Willams & Kremen, 2007) Similar resus
ere reported by Watson etl. (2011 in apple orchards, conlud-
Ing thatthe amount of forest are around the study sites increased
the abundance of polinators (wild bees Furthermore, a recent
review showed that woody cover increased the abundance of a=
sect pollinators and crop pollination serviesinaprculturl areas
[Uishen et al, 2023) For polinator species richness and visa
tion rate, the best model did not include landseape-tevel woody
cover but this could have been because the farming system and,
landscape-evel woody cover were nt enticely independent (gr0-
forestry plots had higher proportions of wood cover inthe sur
Founding landscape, Figure 52) In general, our work suggests that
the woody cover at the landscape scale supports pollinators Thus,
conserving natural forests and coordinating agroforestry farms so
that they increase woody cover on the landscape scale can con:
tribute to improved polination services

"The higher yield in the unbagged than the bogged plants in
both agroforestry and nan-agroforestry pts (bth inthe form
of pods per plant, seeds per pod and seed weight/vield quan-
tity) reinforces the role of insect pollinators in bean production,
(Bartomeus etal, 2014; Douka eta. 2018; Esante et a. 2020)
There was furthermore a greater difference in the number of
seeds per pod between the bagged and untagged flowers in the
agroforestry than in the non agroforestry plots (Table 3: Figure 6)
This s most likely due to the higher abundance, species richness
and visitation rates of flower vistors in agroforestry compared to
ron-agroforestry plots. This results in improved pollination ser-
ices either va the magnitude ofthe service. which linked to
polinator abundance (Castle et al, 2019; Woodcock etal, 2019),
and their visitation rate Bergamo eta 2016: Sagwe eta, 2022),
or by the stability of services which Is more related to polina-
tor lehness (Garibaldi eta, 2011: Hoe et al, 2008: Wintree
etal, 2018) To achieve a more complete picture of how agrafor-
str supports polation future work could consider including a
hand pollination treatment to estimate the overall deficit in pol
nation, as well asa treatment with finely woven bags that are
Impermeable to wind Sagwe etal. 2022)

Even without inet vistation there was higher yield in agrofor
estry than in nom-agroforestry pots higher ill of baged lowers in
agoforestry compared to non-agroforesty plots which implies that
other factors apart from polation services maya be at ply. Fore
ample, sol feriltys often improved in agroforestry systems thrush
ritrogerfixing trees thot ae inteprated in agroforestry farms (Bates
ft al, 2088: Kuyah et al, 2016). Future work s needed to explore the
ditterent mechanisms by which agroforestry fects cop yield

should be noted that the cumulative insect polinator abun:
ance and species richness of 883 and 30 obtained in this study
'as low, but this may at least partly be due to the short lowering
period of the target cop (beans. Sampling completeness curves
suggested that common species were covered well but that
species were missed to some extent. Pollnator communities ap:
peared to difer between farming systems, but this was to some
'extent confounded by the hith variation in community composi
tion among agroforestry sites. Amor intensive sampling regime
might have better captured the diversity of less common polina
tor taxa and could have helpe to disentangle differences in spe
ces composition

5 | CONCLUSIONS

'This study presents strong empirical evidence that agroforestry
systems can support greater polation services, due to higher
Insect polinator abundance, richness and vistation rates than
'non-agrforestry systems. Therefore, agroforestry can be an
important strategy to conserve inseet pollinators, improve crop
veld and positively impact the surrounding environment. We also
{found that landscape-level woody cover. plant richness and tower
abundance ae important for supporting polinators. Thus agro:
forestry hel to be helpful to polinators as long ast increases
flower abundance and diversity and willbe particularly useful fit
{is coordinated so that the wood cover aleo increases at the ind
'scape scale. In sub-Saharan Attica theres growing intrest in im
plementing agroforestry as 2 sustainable agricultural system and
restoration method. This study provides solid evidence that agro
forestry can benefit plinators and polination services and adds
to the body of evidence supporting the adoption of agroforestry
Sharan Arica and elsewhere.
